title: Next.js Mastery Encyclopedia
subtitle: App Router, caching, streaming, runtime boundaries, and full-stack production architecture

sections.intro: Start here if you want the mental model that makes the rest of Next.js click: why the framework exists, how it extends React, and how work is split across build time, request time, server runtime, client runtime, and CDN caching.

blocks.title: What This Chapter Establishes
blocks.html:
This foundation chapter defines the framework story before we touch file conventions or APIs.

  • What Next.js adds on top of React
  • How build, request, server, client, and cache phases differ
  • Why runtime boundaries matter in production apps

sections.intro: This chapter turns the App Router into a system rather than a folder naming exercise: pages, layouts, templates, loading states, errors, route groups, parallel routes, intercepting routes, and route handlers all belong to one routing model.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: The rewrite will consolidate the duplicated App Router material from the old topic into one coherent architecture chapter with file conventions, routing patterns, and layout behavior taught in order.

sections.intro: Next.js is easiest to misuse when the server and client boundary stays fuzzy. This chapter will explain what runs where, what crosses the boundary, and how to avoid shipping unnecessary client JavaScript.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: The full rewrite will cover default server-first rendering, the "use client" boundary, serialization limits, server-only code, and the tradeoffs behind moving logic to the client.

sections.intro: Navigation in Next.js is more than links. Layout persistence, nested route shells, metadata generation, sitemaps, and route-level SEO all interact with the router and rendering model.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: This chapter will combine layout persistence, route transitions, metadata, and crawlability into one learning path so navigation and SEO are taught together instead of scattered across the topic.

sections.intro: Modern Next.js wants data ownership to stay on the server whenever possible. This chapter will teach direct server fetching, parallel data work, request waterfalls, and the heuristics for deciding where data should live.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: The rewrite will turn the surviving server-side data examples into a real chapter on ownership, request coordination, and server-first fetching strategy.

sections.intro: Caching is one of the hardest parts of modern Next.js. This chapter will make the model explicit: Cache Components, use cache, freshness, invalidation, and how stale content appears in real apps.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: This chapter will rebuild the old caching material around the Next 16 model, with practical rules for revalidatePath, revalidateTag, and freshness tradeoffs.

sections.intro: Static rendering, dynamic rendering, streaming, and Partial Prerendering should be understood as one rendering strategy toolbox. This chapter will connect them instead of treating them as isolated buzzwords.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: The rewrite will explain when to choose static, dynamic, streaming, or PPR, how Suspense shapes the user experience, and how shell-versus-hole thinking improves architecture decisions.

sections.intro: Read paths and write paths have different failure modes. This chapter will teach Server Functions, form actions, redirects, validation, optimistic coordination, and safe mutation flows in modern Next.js.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: This chapter will turn scattered server-action material into one mutation chapter built around validation, redirect flows, cache invalidation, and write-path architecture.

sections.intro: Request-time logic deserves its own mental model. This chapter will teach the request path from the browser into proxy.ts, then into route matching, cookies, headers, redirects, and auth gating decisions.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: The rewrite will replace the old middleware-first framing with a request lifecycle chapter centered on proxy.ts, cookies, headers, and where auth checks belong.

sections.intro: Next.js becomes truly valuable when you understand how Route Handlers, databases, auth providers, uploads, webhooks, and background boundaries fit together in one full-stack system.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: This chapter will cover Route Handlers versus Server Functions, ORM and driver tradeoffs, Auth.js integration, and where to draw boundaries around uploads, webhooks, and backend work.

sections.intro: Performance in Next.js is more than Lighthouse scores. This chapter will connect assets, prefetching, hydration cost, bundle weight, Turbopack, and the practical debugging moves that keep large apps responsive.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: The rewrite will combine image and font optimization, build tooling, hydration cost, prefetch behavior, and bundle diagnostics into one performance chapter.

sections.intro: Server rendering and full-stack mutations create real attack surface. This chapter will teach secret boundaries, validation, auth and authz, SSR-specific risks, cache poisoning, and reliability patterns that fail safely.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: The finished chapter will turn scattered warnings into a true hardening guide covering validation, secret handling, request trust boundaries, and production reliability.

sections.intro: Shipping Next.js well means understanding runtimes, hosting assumptions, self-hosting tradeoffs, output modes, adapters, observability, and how the framework behaves outside local development.

blocks.title: Scaffold Focus
blocks.html: This chapter will consolidate deployment and runtime material into one production operations layer covering Vercel, self-hosting, standalone output, adapters, observability, and scale decisions.

sections.intro: The capstone chapter will tie modern Next.js architecture to migration strategy and interview pressure. It will cover Pages Router history, migration choices, debugging tradeoffs, and senior-level system reasoning.

blocks.title: Capstone Scope
blocks.html: This chapter is the temporary home for the interview bank while the topic is being rewritten. As the later tasks progress, questions will be redistributed into the earlier chapters and this capstone will hold the hardest architecture, migration, and debugging prompts.

qa.question: What problem does Next.js solve beyond plain React?
qa.answerHtml: Next.js adds framework-level answers for routing, server rendering, data fetching boundaries, caching, asset optimization, and deployment so teams do not have to assemble those pieces manually around React.

qa.question: What is the App Router?
qa.answerHtml: The App Router is the file-system router built around layouts, nested segments, Server Components, streaming, route handlers, and modern rendering and caching patterns in the app/ directory.

qa.question: How do Server Components differ from Client Components in Next.js?
qa.answerHtml: Server Components run on the server by default and can fetch data or access server-only code without shipping that logic to the browser. Client Components are explicitly marked with "use client" and are used for interactivity, browser APIs, and stateful client-side UI.

qa.question: When does a route become dynamic in modern Next.js?
qa.answerHtml: A route becomes dynamic when its work depends on request-specific data or uncached runtime behavior, such as cookies, headers, truly dynamic fetches, or other per-request signals that prevent static reuse.

qa.question: What does use cache change in the Next.js caching model?
qa.answerHtml: use cache makes caching explicit. Instead of assuming cached behavior by default, it lets you mark work that should be reused and then pair that decision with revalidation and invalidation strategy.

qa.question: What is the difference between a Route Handler and a Server Function?
qa.answerHtml: A Route Handler exposes an HTTP endpoint and is useful for webhooks, uploads, API integrations, and non-UI callers. A Server Function is usually invoked from the app itself, especially forms and UI-driven mutations, and fits best when the caller is already inside the Next.js tree.

qa.question: Why would you choose proxy.ts instead of doing all auth checks during rendering?
qa.answerHtml: proxy.ts is useful for request-time redirects, rewrites, early gating, and policy decisions that should happen before rendering work starts. In-render checks are still useful when access depends on data needed during the render itself.

qa.question: How is Partial Prerendering different from classic SSR or SSG?
qa.answerHtml: Partial Prerendering combines a static shell with dynamic streamed regions. That gives you a route that behaves partly like prerendered content and partly like streamed request-time work, instead of forcing the whole route into one bucket.

qa.question: How do you choose between the Edge runtime and the Node runtime?
qa.answerHtml: Choose Edge for lightweight request-time logic that benefits from low-latency execution close to users, and choose Node when you need the broader server ecosystem, heavier libraries, or capabilities that are not available in the Edge runtime.

qa.question: Why can hydration mismatches still happen in a Next.js app?
qa.answerHtml: Hydration mismatches happen when the client renders different output than the server produced. Common causes include reading browser-only values during render, time-based or random output, and state that depends on client-only APIs before hydration completes.

qa.question: How would you secure a Server Function that mutates data?
qa.answerHtml: Treat it like a trusted server write path: authenticate the caller, authorize the action, validate all input, constrain side effects, handle failures clearly, and trigger revalidation only after the write succeeds.

qa.question: How would you migrate a mature Pages Router app to the App Router safely?
qa.answerHtml: Migrate incrementally. Keep the routing systems separated, move stable route groups first, validate server and client boundaries carefully, compare rendering and cache behavior route by route, and use the migration to simplify duplicated data-fetching and layout code instead of copying it forward blindly.
